mapQuestions.question: Which part of the world is New Zealand in?
mapQuestions.answer: Oceania, in the south-west Pacific Ocean. It is made up of two main islands (the North Island and the South Island) plus lots of smaller ones 🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of New Zealand?
mapQuestions.answer: Wellington, on the southern tip of the North Island. Most people guess Auckland, but Auckland is just the biggest city, not the capital. Wellington is the windiest capital city in the world.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Māori name for New Zealand?
mapQuestions.answer: Aotearoa, which means "the land of the long white cloud". The first Māori explorers spotted clouds stretched over the islands from far out at sea.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in New Zealand?
mapQuestions.answer: Aoraki / Mount Cook in the Southern Alps, 3,724 metres. Sir Edmund Hillary, the first person to climb Mount Everest, trained on this mountain.

mapQuestions.question: How far is New Zealand from its closest big neighbour?
mapQuestions.answer: Around 2,000 km from Australia across the Tasman Sea. New Zealand is one of the most isolated countries on Earth.

foods.name: Hokey pokey ice cream
foods.description: Vanilla ice cream with chunks of crunchy honeycomb toffee. New Zealand's most-loved ice cream flavour.

foods.name: Kiwifruit
foods.description: Small fuzzy brown fruit with bright green (or gold) flesh inside. Named after the kiwi bird. New Zealand grows them all over the country.

foods.name: Pavlova
foods.description: Crisp meringue, fluffy inside, topped with whipped cream and fresh kiwifruit or strawberries. The country argues with Australia about who invented it.

foods.name: Mince and cheese pie
foods.description: A small handheld pie of minced beef and melted cheese in flaky pastry. Sold in every petrol station and bakery.

foods.name: Hāngī
foods.description: A traditional Māori feast cooked in an underground earth oven heated by hot stones. Meat and vegetables steam for hours and taste smoky and tender.

foods.name: L&P
foods.description: Lemon & Paeroa, a fizzy lemony soft drink made in New Zealand and sold everywhere. The slogan: "World famous in New Zealand".

scavengerItems.label: A field of sheep (there are about 5 sheep for every person in NZ)
The silver fern symbol on a sign, shirt or flag
A real kiwifruit growing on a vine or for sale at a market
A Māori carving in wood or stone (often on gateways or buildings)
Steam coming out of the ground, a hot pool, or a mud pool
A rugby field, ball, or someone wearing an All Blacks shirt
Surfers or kids swimming at a beach
A "Lord of the Rings filming location" sign, or a hobbit door
Someone wearing jandals (flip-flops) instead of shoes
A tūī (black bird with a white tuft) or a kererū (huge wood pigeon)
A pounamu (greenstone) pendant on a leather cord around someone's neck
Snow on a mountain peak even in summer

geoMatches.description: The biggest city, on the North Island. Built on 53 volcanoes (most of them sleeping). Known as the "City of Sails" because of all the yachts in the harbour.
The capital, on the southern tip of the North Island. Windiest capital in the world. Home of the Te Papa national museum and lots of cafés.
A geothermal town on the North Island. Geysers, bubbling mud pools, steam rising from drains, and the smell of sulphur in the air. The heartland of Māori culture.
The "adventure capital", on a lake surrounded by mountains in the South Island. Bungy jumping, jet boats, skiing, hiking, ziplines, you name it.
A deep fjord on the South Island's west coast, with sheer cliffs rising 1,200 metres straight out of dark water. One of the wettest places on Earth.
Near the town of Matamata on the North Island, where Peter Jackson built 44 real hobbit holes for the Lord of the Rings films. The set is still standing.

senses.smell: Sulphur drifting through Rotorua streets, fresh pine on a forest walk, salty Tasman air, sheep on a hillside, hot pools steaming, fresh bread from a country bakery…
senses.hear: A tūī making weird clicks and bell notes, sheep bleating across a paddock, the Kiwi accent ("fush and chups"), wind through tussock grass, the haka being chanted, surf on a wild beach…
senses.taste: Cold hokey pokey ice cream on a hot day, sweet pavlova with kiwifruit, salty fish and chips eaten out of paper, hot mince pie, fizzy L&P, smoky hāngī meat…
senses.feel: Icy lake water, hot geothermal mud, soft jandals on bare feet, sticky pine sap, wind whipping your hair on a clifftop, warm sun and cold wind at the same time…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is New Zealand called "the land of the long white cloud"?
stories.body: Around 700 years ago, Polynesian explorers in huge double-hulled canoes set out across the Pacific Ocean from islands thousands of kilometres away. They navigated by stars, by sea birds, and by the way the ocean swelled. According to the most famous story, the great explorer Kupe spotted long streams of white clouds stretched over an unknown land. He named the country Aotearoa, "the land of the long white cloud". The clouds form because moist Pacific air gets pushed up by New Zealand's tall mountains, and even today you can see the long white clouds hanging over the islands when you fly in.

stories.question: What is a kiwi?
stories.body: The kiwi is New Zealand's national bird, about the size of a chicken, with no wings, soft brown feathers like fur, whiskers like a cat, and nostrils right at the tip of its long beak (it is the only bird that smells with its beak). Kiwis can lay an egg that weighs nearly a quarter of their entire body weight, the equivalent of a human giving birth to a 4-year-old. They are nocturnal, so most New Zealanders have never seen a wild one. Kiwis are endangered because rats, stoats, dogs and cats brought by humans love to eat their eggs. So-called "kiwi sanctuaries" all over the country fence out the predators and let kiwis quietly come back.

stories.question: Why is the ground hot?
stories.body: New Zealand sits on the "Ring of Fire", a horseshoe of volcanoes and earthquake zones around the edge of the Pacific Ocean. In the middle of the North Island, two giant tectonic plates grind together underground, and the heat finds its way to the surface as geysers, hot springs, and bubbling mud pools. In the town of Rotorua, steam rises from drains in the street and the air smells of rotten eggs (that is sulphur). Māori people for centuries have cooked food in the steam and bathed in the natural hot pools. Be very careful where you walk: some pools are 100°C, hot enough to cook you.

stories.question: What is the Treaty of Waitangi?
stories.body: In 1840, after British settlers had been arriving in New Zealand for decades, the British Crown and around 540 Māori chiefs signed a treaty at Waitangi, in the Bay of Islands. The Māori version of the treaty said the chiefs would share governance and keep control of their land, fisheries and treasures. The English version said they were handing sovereignty over to Queen Victoria. The two versions said different things, and over the next century most Māori land was lost to settlers, sometimes by force. Today the Treaty of Waitangi is the founding document of modern New Zealand. The country has both English and Māori as official languages, and a special Tribunal that hears Māori claims about the way the Treaty was broken.
stories.thinkingQuestion: When two sides of an agreement say different things, who gets to decide what it really meant? How would you fix it if it happened today?

stories.question: Why is New Zealand wildlife so unusual?
stories.body: Before humans arrived, New Zealand was one of the most isolated places on Earth, with only two species of small bats and a few species of land mammal among the only mammals. Everything else, the birds, the giant moa (now extinct, some stood over 3.5 metres tall), the fish, the lizards, had been alone for tens of millions of years. Many birds lost the ability to fly because there was nothing to escape from. When Māori arrived around 1300 CE and Europeans 500 years later, they brought rats, dogs, cats, possums and stoats. The flightless birds were not afraid, and many species died out within a few centuries. Today New Zealand has the biggest wildlife-protection programme in the world, trying to remove predators from islands and create safe places for kiwi, kakapo (a flightless parrot) and kea (an alpine parrot famous for ripping up cars).

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you have eaten in NZ so far?
Have you tried saying "kia ora" out loud to a stranger?
Mountains, beaches, forests, or geothermal weirdness — which has been your favourite so far?
New Zealand is famous for adventure sports. Would you bungy jump? Jet boat? Skydive?
Aussies and Kiwis tease each other constantly. What's been the most "Kiwi" thing you have noticed?
700 years ago Māori explorers navigated here by stars across the open Pacific. What do you think that journey would have been like?

wordSearch.hint: New Zealand foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Merino or Romney sheep
animals.description: New Zealand has 5 sheep for every person. They're everywhere.

animals.name: Pukeko
animals.description: Bright blue swamp-hen with red beak and big feet, in every wetland and roadside ditch.

animals.name: Tūī
animals.description: Black bird with a white throat-tuft and a wild song of clicks, bells and whistles.

animals.name: New Zealand fur seal
animals.description: On rocky coasts especially Kaikōura and Cape Palliser. Don't get between them and the sea.

animals.name: Kea
animals.description: Mountain parrot in the South Island Alps — clever enough to dismantle a car windscreen wiper for fun.

animals.name: Kiwi bird
animals.description: Nocturnal flightless bird, usually only seen in sanctuaries. National icon.
